facts.fact: Prefix sums reduce range query time complexity from O(s) per query (where s is the number of elements) to O(1) after O(n) preprocessing, making total complexity O(n + q) for n elements and q queries
For a range sum query from index L to R, the result is computed as: prefix[R] - prefix[L-1], or simply prefix[R] if L=0
2D prefix sums extend to matrices using the formula: ans = prefix[xe][ye] - prefix[xe][ys-1] - prefix[xs-1][ye] + prefix[xs-1][ys-1], enabling O(1) rectangular range sum queries
Fenwick Trees (Binary Indexed Trees) achieve O(log N) time for both point updates and range queries with O(N) space, making them more memory-efficient than segment trees which require O(4*N)
Monotonic stacks transform O(n²) nested-loop range query solutions into O(n) single-pass algorithms by maintaining a stack of elements in monotonic order
Sliding window maximum problems can be solved with a monotonic deque in O(n) by maintaining elements in decreasing order of value within the current window
Prefix sums work for counting, not just summing values, making them versatile for problems involving frequency queries and range updates
2D Segment Trees store an entire segment tree at each node to handle the second coordinate, achieving O(log²n) query time and O(log n) update time for 2D range sum queries
Prefix sums can be computed efficiently in parallel, making them a fundamental primitive in parallel algorithm design
Range minimum query (RMQ) preprocessing is a classical problem in algorithm design, with trade-offs between storage S(n), preprocessing time P(n), and query time Q(n)

examples.example: LeetCode 239 (Sliding Window Maximum): Given array [1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7] and window k=3, output [3,3,5,5,6,7]. Monotonic deque solution maintains indices in decreasing order of values, achieving O(n) instead of O(n*k)
LeetCode 304 (Range Sum Query 2D): Build a 2D prefix sum matrix to answer rectangular range sum queries in O(1) time for a static matrix
LeetCode 907 (Sum of Subarray Minimums): Uses monotonic stack to determine, for each element, the previous and next smaller elements, reducing the problem from O(n²) enumeration to O(n) processing
LeetCode 2104 (Sum of Subarray Ranges): Similar to problem 907, uses monotonic stack to efficiently compute the sum of max - min for all subarrays
USACO Silver-level problems regularly use prefix sum arrays to preprocess input for subsequent O(1) range queries, enabling competitive programmers to solve otherwise O(n*q) problems in O(n+q)

analogies.analogy: A prefix sum array is like a speedometer's odometer—instead of counting every mile traveled from the start for each distance query, you just subtract the odometer readings at two points to get the distance between them

Monotonic stacks are like a stack of dinner plates where you only keep plates in decreasing height order—when a taller plate arrives, shorter ones become irrelevant (you pop them) because they can never be the 'next greater' element for anything above them
A 2D prefix sum is to a matrix what a cumulative distribution function (CDF) is to a probability distribution—it lets you answer 'total up to this point' queries in constant time
Segment trees are like a recursive folder structure where leaf nodes store single values and parent nodes store aggregates—each level halves the search space, giving you O(log n) access
A Fenwick tree is like the carry mechanism in addition—each bit position stores partial sums that, when combined correctly, reconstruct any prefix sum query

key_numbers.metric: Range sum query time after preprocessing
Fenwick Tree point update and range query time

key_numbers.unit: time complexity for problems that appear O(n²)

common_misconceptions.misconception: Prefix sums only work for summing numbers
common_misconceptions.reality: Prefix sums work for any associative operation with identity element: summing, counting, XOR, min/max, and any prefix-compatible aggregation

common_misconceptions.misconception: Prefix sums require the entire array to be immutable/preprocessed upfront
common_misconceptions.reality: For mutable arrays with updates, you can use Fenwick Trees or Segment Trees to maintain O(log n) both update and query time, though pure prefix sums are O(1) query only

common_misconceptions.misconception: Segment trees are always better than Fenwick trees
common_misconceptions.reality: Fenwick trees are preferable for range sum and prefix sum problems due to simpler implementation, better cache locality, and lower memory overhead (O(n) vs O(4n))

common_misconceptions.misconception: Monotonic stacks are only for 'next greater element' problems
common_misconceptions.reality: Monotonic stacks solve range query problems (min/max in subarrays), histogram problems, sum of subarray extrema, and any problem where you need next/previous greater/smaller efficiently

common_misconceptions.misconception: 2D prefix sums require O(n²) preprocessing for an n×n matrix
common_misconceptions.reality: 2D prefix sums can be computed in O(n²) preprocessing but queries remain O(1); the trade-off is fixed upfront, unlike dynamic structures

common_misconceptions.misconception: A sliding window monotonic deque requires two separate deques for min and max
common_misconceptions.reality: You can use a single monotonic deque for either min or max; using two deques is optional, just maintains both simultaneously

common_misconceptions.misconception: Prefix sums cannot handle negative numbers
common_misconceptions.reality: Prefix sums work identically with negative numbers; the subtraction formula prefix[R] - prefix[L-1] handles all integer ranges
